reasoning:
The baseline value (presumably Friday, Feb 13's close of 3.68%) is at the lower end of its recent two-week trading range (which averaged ~3.69%). In late January 2026, the Fed held the target rate steady at 3.50%–3.75% (with interest on reserves at 3.65%) citing inflation pressures from tariffs. Markets are currently pricing in only one rate cut for the entirety of 2026, down from previous expectations. Because the easing cycle has paused, the 3-month yield is likely to drift upwards towards the 3.75% upper bound rather than drop, providing an upward bias. The FRED statistical model provided a prior of 0.70 for a short-term increase. 

For the first resolution date (Feb 22), the short horizon and recent floor at 3.68% strongly favors a bounce back above the baseline. For the second date (March 17), the forecast horizon is longer, introducing more uncertainty before the likely mid-March FOMC meeting regarding potential downside macro reports. Adhering to the rule that uncertainty should increase with horizon, the probability for the distant date is pulled closer to 0.5.
